days.title: Court blocks order on federal voter lists and mail ballots
days.summary: A federal judge in Massachusetts blocked parts of a presidential executive order that sought to create a federal voter list and change how mail ballots could be delivered. Oregon and Washington were among the states challenging the order. The challenge argued that the Constitution gives states and Congress—not the president—the main authority to set election rules. The ruling means the order will not affect this year’s general election while the legal fight continues.
days.keyPeople: President, federal judge, Oregon and Washington officials, state election officials, voters, Congress, U.S. Postal Service.
days.coreIssue: Whether the president can use executive power to reshape election administration, or whether that power belongs mainly to states and Congress.

vocabMatch.clue: A presidential directive that sought changes involving federal voter lists and mail ballots.
States challenged whether election administration authority belonged to states/Congress or the president.
A federal judge examined government action and blocked parts of the order.
The dispute involved who has constitutional authority over rules for federal elections.

timeline: The president issued an order involving voter lists and mail ballots
States challenged the order in federal court
A judge blocked key parts of the order
The dispute may continue through appeals or future legislation

days.title: Senate approves war powers resolution challenging presidential action
days.summary: The Senate approved a war powers resolution aimed at blocking continued U.S. military action against Iran unless Congress authorizes it. The vote came after lawmakers raised concerns about the president’s use of military power and the need for Congress to play its constitutional role in decisions about war. The measure still faced additional steps and possible obstacles before becoming binding policy.
days.keyPeople: U.S. senators, president, military officials, voters, party leaders, foreign policy stakeholders.
days.coreIssue: Whether the president can continue military action without clearer congressional authorization.

vocabMatch.clue: Lawmakers debated continued U.S. military action and whether Congress must authorize it.
The story involved disagreement between the president’s military action and Congress’s role.
The Senate used a resolution to challenge or limit executive action.
The article referenced the president’s use of military power.

timeline: The administration used military power in the Iran conflict
Senators debated Congress’s constitutional role
The Senate approved a war powers resolution
Further action may depend on the House, president, courts, or funding decisions

days.title: States could face new SNAP costs under federal policy
days.summary: A new federal policy could require states with high error rates in the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, or SNAP, to pay part of the cost of food benefits beginning in future fiscal years. SNAP has traditionally been a federal benefit program administered with help from the states. Under the new policy, states with higher payment error rates may have to pay a share of benefit costs, while some states with especially high rates could receive delays or additional implementation time.
days.keyPeople: Congress, president, federal agencies, state governments, SNAP recipients, advocacy groups, taxpayers.
days.coreIssue: Whether the federal government should shift more program costs to states when state administration produces high error rates.

vocabMatch.clue: A federal benefit program is administered with help from state governments.
States could be required to share costs if error rates are high.
The story involves money and responsibility shared between national and state governments.
The story focuses on how a national program is carried out by governments.

timeline: Federal law changed future SNAP cost rules
States reviewed error rates and possible financial impacts
Advocacy groups warned about access and state budgets
States may change administration or eligibility decisions

days.title: America 250 events highlight public debate over national identity
days.summary: Communities and government institutions have continued planning events connected to the 250th anniversary of the United States. The celebrations include ceremonies, museum programs, historical exhibits, and public events focused on the founding era. Some organizers have emphasized unity and civic participation, while others have highlighted debates about which parts of American history should receive the most attention during the anniversary year.
days.keyPeople: Federal officials, state and local governments, museums, historians, students, families, civic organizations.
days.coreIssue: How public institutions choose to present the founding era, national identity, and civic participation during a major anniversary.

vocabMatch.clue: Events emphasize civic participation and the role of the people in government.
The anniversary focuses on citizens, representation, and the founding of a republic.
Many founding-era programs discuss rights and ideas from the Declaration of Independence.
Public events invite communities to take part in learning about government and history.

timeline: Federal and local groups planned anniversary events
Museums and schools prepared exhibits and programs
Public debate continued over how to present the founding era
Communities held events tied to civic life and history

days.title: Supreme Court term keeps focus on limits of government power
days.summary: The Supreme Court has continued issuing decisions at the end of its term on disputes involving government authority, individual rights, and the power of public officials. The Court’s decisions can affect how laws are interpreted and how national and state governments act. Several rulings from the term have drawn attention from lawmakers, advocacy groups, state officials, and voters because they shape the meaning of constitutional limits in current policy disputes.
days.keyPeople: Supreme Court justices, lower courts, Congress, president, state governments, advocacy groups, voters.
days.coreIssue: How Supreme Court decisions define the limits of government power and interpret constitutional principles.

vocabMatch.clue: The Court interprets laws and government actions in light of the Constitution.
The article focuses on constitutional limits on government authority.
The Court decides what constitutional language means in current disputes.
Court decisions can guide how future cases and laws are handled.

timeline: Legal disputes reached the Supreme Court
Justices heard arguments and reviewed laws
The Court issued decisions near the end of its term
Officials and groups responded to the rulings
